overview.description: A drawer is a type of panel that slides in from the side of the screen to provide additional content or functionality.
overview.purpose: A drawer is a type of panel that slides in from the side of the screen to provide additional content or functionality.
whenToUse: When you need to display supplementary content without navigating away from the current view.
whenNotToUse: When you need to display critical information that requires immediate attention.

advanced.AccordionInDrawer:
// JS import "@maersk-global/mds-components-core-accordion"; import "@maersk-global/mds-components-core-accordion-item"; import "@maersk-global/mds-components-core-button"; import "@maersk-global/mds-components-core-drawer"; import "@maersk-global/mds-components-core-icon"; import "@maersk-global/mds-components-core-tooltip"; import "@maersk-global/mds-components-core-tag"; const toggleDrawer = () => { const drawer = document.querySelector('mc-drawer'); if (!drawer) return; drawer.open = !drawer.open; }; // CSS mc-drawer::part(footer) { justify-content: flex-start; } // HTML Open drawer with Accordion Invoice Date: July 2, 2025

Vue3.basic:
<mc-drawer
  position="left"
  customsize="value"
  dimension="small"
  fit="small"
  nonmodal
  nopadding
  disablestickyfooter
  open
  backdropcloseactiondisabled
  @closed="yourEventHandler(e)"
  @closing="yourEventHandler(e)"
  @opened="yourEventHandler(e)"
  @opening="yourEventHandler(e)"
  @click="yourEventHandler(e)"
  hasheadingslot
  hasbodyslot
  hasfooterslot>
</mc-drawer>

VanillaJs.minimal:
<script type="module"
  src="./node_modules/@maersk-global/mds-components-core-drawer/index.js"></script>

<mc-drawer>
</mc-drawer>

<script>
  document.querySelector('mc-drawer').addEventListener('closed', (e) => yourEventHandler(e));
  document.querySelector('mc-drawer').addEventListener('closing', (e) => yourEventHandler(e));
  document.querySelector('mc-drawer').addEventListener('opened', (e) => yourEventHandler(e));
  document.querySelector('mc-drawer').addEventListener('opening', (e) => yourEventHandler(e));
  document.querySelector('mc-drawer').addEventListener('click', (e) => yourEventHandler(e));
</script>

props.name: customsize
props.description: Sets the custom size of the drawer. If the position is set to left or right, the width will be adjusted. If the position is set to top or bottom, the height will be adjusted. You can also set the custom size for different breakpoints by passing an object with the breakpoint sizes i.e. { xs: '100%', sm: '400px', md: '500px', lg: '600px', xl: '50%' }

props.name: dimension
props.description:
The dimension of the drawer.
    The dimension depends on the position of the drawer and are as follows:
    
small:
    
- left: `width: 320px, height: 100dvh`
    
- right: `width: 320px, height: 100dvh`
    
- top: `height: 320px, width: 100dvw`
    
- bottom: `height: 320px, width: 100dvw`


medium:
    
- left: `width: 480px, height: 100dvh`
    
- right: `width: 480px, height: 100dvh`
    
- top: `height: 480px, width: 100dvw`
    
- bottom: `height: 480px, width: 100dvw`


large:
    
- left: `width: 800px, height: 100dvh`
    
- right: `width: 800px, height: 100dvh`
    
- top: `height: 800px, width: 100dvw`
    
- bottom: `height: 800px, width: 100dvw`

props.name: nonmodal
props.description: If true, the drawer will open as a non-modal drawer, allowing page interaction.

props.name: nopadding
props.description: If true, the drawer content will have no padding.

props.name: disablestickyfooter
props.description: Disables the sticky footer when scrolling content.

props.name: backdropcloseactiondisabled
props.description: Disables closing the drawer when clicking outside of it.

props.name: autofocus
props.description: Add `autofocus` attiribute to an element inside of drawer to set focus on it when drawer is opened

props.name: hasheadingslot
props.description:
**For server-side rendering only (e.g., Next.js).**


Set to true when providing content to the heading slot. Not needed for client-side rendering as the slot is detected automatically.

props.name: hasbodyslot
props.description:
**For server-side rendering only (e.g., Next.js).**


Set to true when providing content to the default body slot. Not needed for client-side rendering as the slot is detected automatically.

props.name: hasfooterslot
props.description:
**For server-side rendering only (e.g., Next.js).**


Set to true when providing content to footer/action slots. Not needed for client-side rendering as the slot is detected automatically.

events.name: closed
events.description: The closed event gets dispatched once the drawer is closed.

events.name: closing
events.description: The closing event gets dispatched once the drawer is closing.

events.name: opened
events.description: The opened event gets dispatched once the drawer is opened.

events.name: opening
events.description: The opening event gets dispatched once the drawer is opening.

events.name: click
events.description: The click event gets dispatched when clicked on the dialogaction button within a drawer.

slots.description: Use the default slot, when you want to pass body as HTML to the drawer. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
Use the heading named slot, when you want to pass heading as HTML to the drawer. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
slots.name: heading

slots.description: Use the footer named slot, when you want to pass footer as HTML to the drawer. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
slots.name: footer

types.name: Mcdialogaction
types.jsdoc: Indicates the action that was taken when the drawer was closed.

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the the dialog element(parent). Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: dialog

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the header of the drawer. Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: header

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the wrapper of the drawer content. Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: body-wrapper

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the footer of the drawer. Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: footer

guidelines.title: Drawer
guidelines.description: A drawer, also known as a slide-out panel is used for secondary content and functionality.

best-practice: Use drawers for menus, settings, filters and contextual options.
Position the drawer consistently across the website or application.
Don’t overload the drawer, keep the content simple and focused.
Don’t use a drawer as the primary navigation on desktop.
Avoid using the bottom drawer on desktop.
usage:
Use the drawer for secondary content and functionality that do not have to be visible at all times. 
The most common use cases are menus, settings, filters, contextual actions and tools. 
Drawers can also be used for temporary content such as notifications and chats.

Drawers help save space and create a cleaner UI by hiding secondary information. Avoid using drawers for primary navigation in desktop applications if space allows for a more permanent sidebar or header navigation.
placement: Drawers are typically placed on the left or right side of the screen, but can also be placed in the top or the bottom of the screen.
Ensure that the same type of drawer is placed consistently across the website or application.
content:
Ensure the content inside the drawer is well-organized and scannable. Use headings, dividers, or sections to break up content logically. 
Only include the information necessary for the task at hand e.g., menu items, filters.

designPrinciples.title: Spacing
designPrinciples.description: Consistent spacing creates groups, rhythm and good scan-ability.

use-4-pixel-spacing: The base unit for spacing is 4 pixels, subsequent units are multiples of 4 (such as 8, 12, 16, etc.). These units should be used consistently for all paddings, margins, gutters, typography, and layouts to ensure a cohesive appearance and user experience across all Maersk digital products.
exceptions-to-4-pixel-spacing: When creating components, finer control is needed for spacing elements. Three acceptable exceptions here are 2, 6, and 10 pixels.
components: Relate the component elements and still keep them distinct by using space units dervided from the 4-pixel base spacing. These space units will make aligning and using non-core components with core ones easier.
patterns: A recognisable pattern is created when consistent spacing is used to establish a visual rhythm and relationship between the pattern elements.

Pattern spacing needs to be coherent which means not all space units need to be the same. Several spacing units can help establish better visual balance when dealing with differently-sized UI elements in the pattern.
layouts: Visual hierarchy in layouts requires well spaced regions and UI elements. Spacing units can control the information density of any application layout. Use them to provide the right level for the context and users' expectation.
information-density: Space is the element that controls the information density of any interface. Adjusting the space between blocks on information creates a visual rhythm and hierarchy.

High information density can be disorienting and overwhelming if spacing does not allow the eyes to rest and let people process what they see. People may notice the grouped elements but not process each individual item.

We recommend reading the <PageLink path="/design-language/component-fit/">Fit guidelines</PageLink> to help you make an informed decision what spacing units will be most suitable for your layout composition.

relatedGuidelines.title: Accessibility
relatedGuidelines.description: Improving the user experience for everyone.

what-is-accessibility?: Accessibility means that websites, tools and technologies are designed and developed in a way that makes it possible for people with disabilities to use them. Accessibility also benefits people without disabilities for example people with temporary disabilities or situational disabilities.
Accessibility helps people perceive, understand and interact with our digital products and it takes auditory, cognitive, neurological, physical, speech and visual limitations into consideration.

But accessibility is also a mindset and a change in the way we think and talk about digital products. It is a cultural change that cannot be made by one team only. We, therefore, urge all our colleagues as well as top management to get involved and share awareness about this very important topic. We need your support!
why-is-accessibility-important?: One of the main myths about accessibility is that it only concerns a small group of people with severe disabilities. This assumption is far from the reality and according to the World Health Organisation (WHO), more than 1 billion people are living with some level of impairment that affects the way they interact with digital products and access information online.
When our websites, apps and other digital products are designed badly, they create barriers that exclude people and make access to our offerings cumbersome and in some cases not possible at all.
being-inaccessible-is-a-problem: It is an ethical problem when we exclude certain groups of people from our products and services.

It is a legal problem, especially in the US where companies are being sued if they are not accessible, which could have financial consequences in terms of fines as well as affecting the brand negatively.

It is a big business problem - if we choose to exclude a very big group of potential customers because they have a disability.
When we design and build products with accessibility in mind, we not only open up our products and experiences for more people, but our products start to become more human - in the sense that they reflect the diversity of society and become much more flexible and robust to accommodate many different needs and use cases.
